interest: The interest behind this claim spans equity advocates, low-SES and Roma families, comprehensive-education reformers, and the digital-economy talent interest, all of whom want the age-10 sorting event replaced because it is identified as the primary veto point for disadvantaged pupils.
value: Equity (giving every child a level competitive window to age 14) is in tension with excellence/parental choice (the high-SES claim to early differentiation and selective placement).
fear: The driving fear is that a child with no advocate is permanently labelled and disadvantaged at age 10 before their ability is settled, foreclosing life chances for a generation.
assumption: It assumes the cross-national correlation between early tracking and SES gradients is causal and transferable to Hungary, and that removing the formal sorting event actually reduces sorting rather than merely relocating it to school choice or non-state maintainers.
empirical_uncertainty: The cross-national fact-base is contested-to-moderate: the tracking/SES-gradient association is documented (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006; Pekkarinen et al. 2009; Brunello & Checchi 2007) at moderate-to-strong strength, but no Hungarian data exists, causal transferability is unproven, and the sorting-migration counter-mechanism is flagged by the scenario itself as the largest single risk and left unresolved.
